title: Social Anxiety Thought Challenger
description: Use this worksheet to identify and challenge the automatic negative thoughts that drive social anxiety. Work through each step to develop more balanced, realistic interpretations of social situations.
instructions: When you notice social anxiety rising — before, during, or after a social situation — work through each step below. Start by describing the situation, identify your automatic thought, examine the evidence, and develop a balanced alternative.

fields.label: 1. Situation — What happened? Where were you? Who was there?
fields.placeholder: e.g., Walking into a meeting room where people were already talking...

fields.label: 2. Automatic Thought — What went through your mind?
fields.placeholder: e.g., 'Everyone stopped talking because of me. They were probably talking about me.'

fields.label: 3. Emotion Intensity — How anxious/embarrassed did you feel? (0 = not at all, 100 = most intense ever)
4. Thinking Trap — Which cognitive pattern best fits?

options.label: Spotlight Effect — Overestimating how much others notice
Mind Reading — Assuming others think negatively of me
Perfectionism — Impossible standards for social performance
Illusion of Transparency — 'They can see I'm anxious'
Emotional Reasoning — 'I feel awkward, so I must seem awkward'
Fortune Telling — Predicting negative social outcomes

fields.label: 5. Evidence For — What supports this thought? (Use facts, not feelings)
fields.placeholder: e.g., The room got quieter when I walked in...

fields.label: 6. Evidence Against — What contradicts this thought?
fields.placeholder: e.g., People often pause when someone enters. They smiled at me. No one made negative comments. I've entered rooms many times without being judged.

fields.label: 7. Balanced Thought — What is a more realistic interpretation?
fields.placeholder: e.g., 'People pausing when someone enters is normal. It doesn't mean they were talking about me. Most people were probably glad to see me.'

fields.label: 8. Re-rate Emotion — How anxious/embarrassed do you feel now? (0 = not at all, 100 = most intense ever)

distortionsList.name: Spotlight Effect
distortionsList.description: Overestimating how much others notice about you — feeling like you're under a spotlight that highlights your flaws and anxiety.

distortionsList.name: Mind Reading
distortionsList.description: Assuming you know what others are thinking about you — almost always assuming negative judgments.

distortionsList.name: Probability Overestimation
distortionsList.description: Overestimating the likelihood that feared outcomes will occur, treating 'might' as 'will'.

distortionsList.name: Catastrophizing
distortionsList.description: Assuming that if something goes slightly wrong socially, the consequences will be devastating and permanent.

distortionsList.name: Perfectionism
distortionsList.description: Setting unrealistically high standards for social performance — all-or-nothing thinking about social encounters.

distortionsList.name: Illusion of Transparency
distortionsList.description: Believing your internal anxiety is visible to others — that they can 'see through you'.

distortionsList.name: Emotional Reasoning
distortionsList.description: Believing that because you feel anxious or awkward, you must appear that way to others.

distortionsList.name: Fortune Telling
distortionsList.description: Predicting negative social outcomes without evidence: 'I know the party will go badly.'

disclaimer: This worksheet is for educational purposes only and is not a substitute for professional mental health treatment. If you are in crisis, contact the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline by calling or texting 988.
